scenario: Apex Logistics evaluates 7000 shipments per day through a Camunda 8.8 routing process backed by DMN. A new hire is asked to choose a hit policy and has heard that some policies guarantee a single result while others can return several. The architect wants the team to know the precise grouping so they pick a single-result policy for the routing decision and a multi-result policy for an audit decision.
question: Which grouping correctly classifies the supported hit policies by whether they return at most one satisfied rule or possibly several?

options.text: FIRST and COLLECT return at most one satisfied rule, while UNIQUE, ANY, and RULE ORDER can return multiple satisfied rules
UNIQUE and COLLECT return at most one satisfied rule, while ANY, FIRST, and RULE ORDER can return multiple satisfied rules
UNIQUE, ANY, and RULE ORDER return at most one satisfied rule, while FIRST and COLLECT can return multiple satisfied rules
UNIQUE, ANY, and FIRST return at most one satisfied rule, while RULE ORDER and COLLECT can return multiple satisfied rules

a.text: Incorrect. FIRST is documented as a single-rule policy, and COLLECT is documented as a multi-rule policy, so the grouping swaps both sides of the split.

b.text: Incorrect. UNIQUE is single-rule by definition and FIRST returns only the first satisfied rule, so placing them on the multi-rule side contradicts the documented role of each.

c.text: Incorrect. RULE ORDER is explicitly listed alongside COLLECT as a policy that can return multiple satisfied rules, not as a single-rule policy.

d.text: Correct. The role-of-a-hit-policy section states that UNIQUE, ANY, and FIRST always return a maximum of one satisfied rule, while RULE ORDER and COLLECT can return multiple satisfied rules.

explanation: The hit policy page splits the supported policies into two families: UNIQUE, ANY, and FIRST always yield at most one satisfied rule in the result, while RULE ORDER and COLLECT can return several satisfied rules.
